claim: 95% of dogs who wander beyond safe zones travel at most 1.8 miles (≈2 km) from home, per Fi GPS-collar data.
stance_target: 1-2 mile search radius captures vast majority of lost dog locations
scope_conditions: Fi GPS collar data (US), 2025 report by HASS; represents dogs that triggered safe-zone alerts
quoted_support: 95% of dogs who wander beyond their designated safe zones travel at most 1.8 miles (≈ 2 km) away from home

claim: Average recovery distance for lost dogs is 0.13 miles (700 feet / ~2 city blocks) from home.
stance_target: hyper-local search (within 2 blocks) should be the first search action for any lost dog
scope_conditions: Fi GPS collar data, 2025; average across dogs recovered after safe-zone breach
quoted_support: on average, lost dogs are recovered just 0.13 miles from home. That's about 700 feet, or a little over two city blocks.

claim: 60% of stray dogs reunited with owners after entering a shelter were found less than 1 mile from home; 42% were spotted just one block away.
stance_target: proximity-first search strategy, consistent with Fi GPS data and Lord 2007b
scope_conditions: HASS shelter data, 2017-2021; US; citing Kremer 2019 for 42% figure
quoted_support: 60% of lost dogs that were reunited with their owner after entering a shelter were picked up less than 1 mile from home... 42% spotted just a single block away (Kremer, 2019).

claim: Finder-to-Foster programs (where finders temporarily hold lost dogs near the point of discovery) double or triple return-to-home rates versus direct shelter surrender.
stance_target: proximity-based retention of found dogs as superior alternative to shelter intake
quoted_support: return-to-home rates doubling or tripling, and often the dog avoids the shelter altogether (Human Animal Support Services, 2022).
